games.explanation: Nolan McLean's shiny surface stats hide a real problem against right-handed bats, and Atlanta happens to run out a lineup full of them tonight. The splits are ugly in the direction that matters: righties are barreling him up at a double-digit clip, and Olson and Baldwin are exactly the profile that punishes a young arm still learning to command the zone to that side. Perez isn't inspiring, but Soto aside, this Mets order isn't the murderers row the price implies, and a 93-degree night with the wind blowing in caps the ceiling on any Queens comeback. The market is paying full freight for McLean's ERA and shrugging at the platoon math. At -118 on the better Elo team, you take the arithmetic.

Luinder Avila's numbers look like an ace's until you notice he's faced fewer hitters than most guys see in a homestand, and the Phillies are about to introduce him to a lineup that eats command-shaky righties for breakfast. Schwarber alone is the kind of problem a 56-batter sample doesn't prepare you for, with a barrel rate north of 20% that turns any mislocated fastball into a scoreboard update. Nola isn't vintage, but he's a known quantity against a Kansas City order that leans heavily on Witt and hopes the rest figures itself out. The market is pricing Avila like the strikeout rate is real and the walks aren't, and that's the crack we're stepping through at -136.

The market is treating this like a coin flip because it sees "Marlins in Oakland" and stops thinking, which is exactly where the edge lives. Miami is the significantly better team by Elo, and they happen to be running out the pitcher who tilts the matchup even further, an Eury Pérez arsenal that misses bats at a 27 percent clip and holds hitters to a sub-.290 xwOBA. The Athletics lineup outside of Kurtz is either strikeout-prone or overmatched, and Gelof punching out in nearly half his plate appearances is not the guy you want anchoring a comeback. Getting plus money on the stronger roster with the sharper starter is the entire ticket, and +106 is the market politely handing it over.

picks.explanation: Nolan McLean's shiny surface stats hide a real problem against right-handed bats, and Atlanta happens to run out a lineup full of them tonight. The splits are ugly in the direction that matters: righties are barreling him up at a double-digit clip, and Olson and Baldwin are exactly the profile that punishes a young arm still learning to command the zone to that side. Perez isn't inspiring, but Soto aside, this Mets order isn't the murderers row the price implies, and a 93-degree night with the wind blowing in caps the ceiling on any Queens comeback. The market is paying full freight for McLean's ERA and shrugging at the platoon math. At -118 on the better Elo team, you take the arithmetic.

Cleveland getting laid a run and a half against a White Sox team the market has finally noticed exists feels like a leftover from April pricing. These clubs are separated by a rounding error in Elo, the projected margin is half a run, and Bibee is a fine pitcher rather than the kind of arm who buries a lineup and covers a big number. Teel and Benintendi both profile well against him from the left side, and Chicago only needs to lose by one to cash. Getting plus juice on the run line in a coin-flip game is the entire argument.

Luinder Avila's numbers look like an ace's until you notice he's faced fewer hitters than most guys see in a homestand, and the Phillies are about to introduce him to a lineup that eats command-shaky righties for breakfast. Schwarber alone is the kind of problem a 56-batter sample doesn't prepare you for, with a barrel rate north of 20% that turns any mislocated fastball into a scoreboard update. Nola isn't vintage, but he's a known quantity against a Kansas City order that leans heavily on Witt and hopes the rest figures itself out. The market is pricing Avila like the strikeout rate is real and the walks aren't, and that's the crack we're stepping through at -136.

Milwaukee is the better team, nobody's arguing that, but this line is priced like the Brewers are going to run away with a game they've never actually run away with. Sproat has faced fewer than a hundred hitters in the big leagues and is walking guys at close to an 11% clip, which is a problem when the top of Arizona's order is Marte, Perdomo, and Carroll doing damage against righties all season. E-Rod isn't sharp, but he doesn't need to be sharp to keep this within a run, he just needs to grind through five while his lineup pecks at a rookie who hasn't shown he can put anyone away. The sim lands on a one-run game, and getting a run and a half at -140 in a projected coin flip is the entire reason this ticket exists. Take the cushion.

The market is treating this like a coin flip because it sees "Marlins in Oakland" and stops thinking, which is exactly where the edge lives. Miami is the significantly better team by Elo, and they happen to be running out the pitcher who tilts the matchup even further, an Eury Pérez arsenal that misses bats at a 27 percent clip and holds hitters to a sub-.290 xwOBA. The Athletics lineup outside of Kurtz is either strikeout-prone or overmatched, and Gelof punching out in nearly half his plate appearances is not the guy you want anchoring a comeback. Getting plus money on the stronger roster with the sharper starter is the entire ticket, and +106 is the market politely handing it over.

The board sees the Dodgers as the better team and prices this like a coronation, but a run and a half is a lot to give when the projected margin is a field goal at most. San Diego's simulated loss is by three-tenths of a run, which is the definition of a game that shouldn't require insurance to bet the dog. Sheehan misses bats but walks lefties at a 12.7% clip, and Sears, for all his flaws, is capable of the six-inning quiet start that keeps a Padres lineup fronted by Tatis and Machado in striking distance. Getting a coin flip disguised as an underdog at plus juice on the runline is the entire point of doing this.
